nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral-rich reef slopes and in lagoons, the Fu Manchu Lionfish is a secretive species that spends most daylight hours tucked into caves, overhangs, and rubble. It emerges at dusk to hunt small fish and crustaceans.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
open swimming space for ambush behaviour

diet.notes: The Fu Manchu Lionfish is an obligate carnivore that feeds on small fish and crustaceans in the wild. Many specimens arrive refusing prepared food and may require live ghost or feeder shrimp to initiate feeding; once eating reliably, most can be gradually weaned onto thawed mysis, krill, silversides, and chopped marine fish or shellfish. Long-term diets of freshwater feeder fish such as rosy reds or goldfish have been reported to cause fatty-liver disease, so these should be reserved for emergency situations and replaced with marine-origin foods as soon as possible.

goodWith: larger semi-aggressive fish that cannot fit in its mouth
avoidWith: small fish that fit in the mouth (chromis, small gobies, cardinalfish)
ornamental shrimp and other small crustaceans
other Fu Manchu Lionfish in modest tanks
very aggressive tankmates that out-compete it at feeding time

summary: The Fu Manchu Lionfish is a small, secretive Indo-Pacific species with bold red, white, and black banding and distinctive feeler-like appendages beneath the chin. It carries venomous spines, preys on small fish and shrimp, and is often slow to accept prepared foods.
careNotes:
The Fu Manchu Lionfish is one of the smaller members of the Dendrochirus genus, typically reaching 10–13 cm at maturity, but is generally regarded as the most demanding of the lionfishes commonly available in the trade. It is strictly nocturnal and cryptic, spending most daylight hours tucked under rocks or hanging upside-down inside overhangs, so the aquascape should provide several shaded caves with open lanes in front of them where the fish can observe and ambush prey.

Tank size recommendations often cite 30–40 gallons (114–150 L) as a minimum, but the fish's predatory behaviour, its need for swimming room around hiding spots, and the heavy waste load that comes with a piscivore's diet make a tank of around 120 L with strong protein skimming and mechanical filtration a more realistic starting point.

The dorsal, pelvic, and anal spines are venomous. Stings produce intense, localised pain; the standard first aid is immersion of the affected area in hot water at around 45 °C for 30–90 minutes, as heat helps break down the venom. Anyone with allergies or cardiac conditions should seek medical attention after a sting. Care is needed when working around the tank during maintenance, net work, or rockwork rearrangement.

The Fu Manchu Lionfish is not safe with small invertebrates. Any fish or crustacean small enough to fit in its mouth — and the mouth is surprisingly large for the fish's overall size — is likely to be treated as prey. It is generally compatible with corals, but small ornamental shrimp, gobies, cardinalfish, and similarly sized fish should be avoided as tankmates.

Feeding is the main husbandry challenge. Many specimens arrive refusing prepared food and need live ghost or feeder shrimp to begin eating. Once feeding reliably, most can be weaned gradually onto thawed mysis, krill, silversides, and chunks of marine fish or shellfish, ideally offered on feeding tongs. Long-term feeding on freshwater feeder fish has been associated with fatty-liver disease and should be avoided; vitamin-enriched marine foods are a better long-term staple.

The older scientific name Dendrochirus biocellatus remains the name most widely used in the aquarium trade and in hobbyist husbandry resources, though the species has been reclassified and appears under Nemapterois biocellatus in some current taxonomic databases.
breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. Pelagic spawner in the wild; eggs released in a buoyant gelatinous mass. No documented captive-breeding protocol.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care Level: Moderate; min tank 30 gal; temp 72-78 F; pH 8.1-8.4; SG 1.020-1.025; dKH 8-12; max ~4 in; semi-aggressive; reef-safe with caution; venomous dorsal spines. Controller-supplied root URL (liveaquaria.com) 404'd; navigated to actual product page 231 via web search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Family Scorpaenidae; max 13.0 cm TL; depth 1-40 m; Indo-Pacific from Mauritius/Reunion to Society Islands, north to S. Japan, south to Scott Reef; trophic level 3.7; feeds on small fishes and crustaceans; venomous spines; ocelli on soft dorsal. FishBase currently accepts the senior name Nemapterois biocellatus; the trade still uses Dendrochirus biocellatus, which is retained here as scientificName with the synonym recorded in alsoKnownAs.

additional.name: Fishkeeper (UK)
additional.notes: Adult ~10 cm; depth to 40 m; temp 23-27 C; pH 8.1-8.4; SG 1.020-1.025; nocturnal/cryptic; eats small fish and ornamental shrimp; will not harm corals; venomous spines; notoriously difficult to feed initially.

additional.name: Aquatic Community Lionfish Guide
additional.notes: Largest measured 13 cm; temp 72-78 F (22-25.5 C); pH 8.1-8.4; SG 1.020-1.025; ~30 gal (~115 L) minimum; semi-aggressive; reef-safe with caution; pelvic, pectoral and dorsal fins venomous; cryptic, mostly hides during day.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Temp 22-26 C; pH 8.1-8.4; SG 1.020-1.025; semi-aggressive/territorial, solitary; live or frozen shrimp and small fish; intermediate-to-advanced. Lists adult size as 20 cm which disagrees with FishBase's 13 cm TL maximum and was discarded as an outlier.
